meta.name: Pulmonary Embolism — Post-Operative Sudden Dyspnea, S1Q3T3, Supportive Care and Recognition

patient.name: Sandra Reyes
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden, severe shortness of breath that hit her like a wall about 25 minutes ago when she stood up from the couch to walk to the kitchen, with sharp right-sided chest pain that worsens with each breath.

history.hpi: 52-year-old female, 5 days post-op from a laparoscopic total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy for fibroids. She has been mostly resting in a recliner since discharge, getting up only to use the bathroom and eat. Her surgeon's instructions called for short walks every 2 hours, but with the abdominal soreness she has been less active than recommended. About 25 minutes ago she stood up from the couch to walk to the kitchen and was immediately struck with severe shortness of breath she describes as 'like the air just wouldn't come in.' She felt her heart racing, became lightheaded, sat back down hard, and developed a sharp right-sided chest pain that intensifies with deep inspiration ('I can feel where it hurts when I breathe in'). Her husband noticed her lips were 'dusky' and called 911. Her right calf has felt 'a little tight and warm' for the past day, which she dismissed as 'getting old.' She is a former smoker (quit 4 years ago, 20 pack-years) and takes no estrogen-containing medications. No personal or family history of clotting disorders that she knows of.
pastMedical: Status post laparoscopic total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingectomy 5 days ago (for symptomatic uterine fibroids)
Former tobacco use — 20 pack-years, quit 4 years ago
No known clotting disorder; no personal or family history of DVT/PE
medications: Oxycodone 5 mg PO q4–6 hr PRN (post-op, taken twice yesterday and once this morning)
allergies: Penicillin (rash as a child — never re-challenged)
history.lastOralIntake: Coffee and toast about 90 minutes ago
history.events: 5 days of post-operative bedrest with limited ambulation; right calf tightness/warmth ×24 hr; sudden severe dyspnea and pleuritic right-sided chest pain ~25 minutes ago when standing from couch. No syncope. Husband called 911 when he noticed her dusky lips. No trauma, no recent travel.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 52-year-old female, complaint of sudden shortness of breath and chest pain at home. Conscious; husband reports she's struggling to breathe. ALS response.
scene.location: Living room of a two-story home. Patient sitting upright in a recliner with the leg rest down; husband standing by the front door. Recovery items visible — cane, hospital discharge folder, ice pack on her abdomen.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Husband cooperative, has the surgical discharge paperwork in hand. Family dog secured in another room.
scene.bystanders: Husband (primary historian) confirms timeline: 5 days post-op, mostly resting, sudden dyspnea this morning when standing. He noticed her lips looked dusky. He brought her surgical discharge paperwork and pill bottles.
visualCues: Middle-aged woman sitting upright in a recliner, breathing rapidly with shallow breaths
Right hand pressed lightly against her right lower ribs as she breathes
Surgical discharge folder on her lap, abdominal binder visible under her shirt
Right calf visibly slightly larger than left when she lifts her loose pant leg; mild warmth and tenderness over the calf
No oxygen equipment in the house

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 52-year-old woman 5 days post-op (laparoscopic hysterectomy) in obvious respiratory distress with classic PE risk factors: recent surgery, immobility, obesity, prior tobacco. Sudden-onset dyspnea, pleuritic right-sided chest pain, sinus tachycardia at 122, BP 96/62, SpO2 86% on room air, and a unilaterally tender/warm right calf. Lung exam is CLEAR — no rales, no wheeze, no crackles — which is itself a clue (severe hypoxia with clear lungs and risk factors for VTE = PE until proven otherwise). 12-lead shows sinus tachycardia with the classic S1Q3T3 pattern and an incomplete RBBB suggesting right-heart strain. The teaching point is recognition of PE pattern, supportive oxygenation, cautious fluid resuscitation, and rapid transport to a CTPA-capable ED — NOT nitroglycerin, NOT diuretics, NOT bronchodilators.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent — speaking 3–4 words at a time
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 30, shallow with mild splinting on right; chest wall mildly tender at right lower ribs with deep breath; lungs CLEAR bilaterally — no rales, no wheeze, no rhonchi
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong rapid radial pulse; warm; cap refill 2 sec; right calf is mildly warm and slightly larger than left

skin.color: dusky lips and fingertips, otherwise pale

otherFindings: 12-lead: sinus tachycardia 122, S1Q3T3 pattern (S in I, Q and inverted T in III), incomplete RBBB — right-heart strain
Lungs CLEAR despite SpO2 86% — supports PE over CHF/asthma/pneumonia
Right calf tightness/warmth — possible DVT source
EtCO2 28 mmHg — low for tachypnea (V/Q mismatch + hypoperfusion)
Risk factors: 5 days post-op, immobile, obesity, prior tobacco — high pretest probability
Pathway: O2, gentle IV fluids, supportive transport — rule out PE on CTPA at ED
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (short phrases, splinting) Just stood up… and couldn't breathe. Feels like… stabbing. Right side.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize PE pattern: post-op, immobility, sudden dyspnea, clear lungs, calf findings
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Inclusion Criteria 2d + Educational pearls — obstructive shock includes pulmonary embolism, tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade. History (post-op, immobile) plus exam (clear lungs, hypoxia, unilateral calf finding) drive the differential.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Potential etiologies of shock: Hypovolemic (hemorrhagic or non-hemorrhagic). Distributive (sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic, overdose, endocrine). Cardiogenic (cardiomyopathy, dysrhythmia, valve disorder). Obstructive (pulmonary embolism (PE), tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade).

expectedActions.shortAction: High-flow O2 via NRB; titrate to SpO2 94–98%; escalate to BVM-assisted if WOB worsens
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Treatment 1a — supplemental O2 escalating from NC to reach 94–98%; MATC Shock Treatment 2 — oxygen targeted to 94–98%.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Give supplemental oxygen for dyspnea to a target of 94–98% saturation. Escalate from a nasal cannula as needed to reach this goal.

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead EKG; identify S1Q3T3 / RBBB pattern of right-heart strain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Monitoring 2c — 12-lead may be indicated for dysrhythmia/ischemia/CHF presentation; PE specifically can show right-heart strain pattern that helps prehospital recognition.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: 12-lead EKG may be indicated to assess for dysrhythmia or ischemia, particularly in patients with risk factors for coronary artery disease and/or presentation consistent with CHF.

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access ×2; cardiac monitor; SpO2 + EtCO2 (low EtCO2 with tachypnea = poor perfusion / V/Q mismatch)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Treatment 4 + Respiratory Distress Patient Safety 1 — EtCO2 <25 may signal poor perfusion; normal EtCO2 with tachypnea/distress is a marker of impending failure; routine pulse ox + EtCO2 in respiratory complaints.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pulse oximetry and EtCO2 (reading of less than 25 mmHg may be sign of poor perfusion). Establish IV access. If unable to obtain within two attempts or less than 90 seconds, place an IO needle.

expectedActions.shortAction: Examine calves bilaterally — unilateral swelling/warmth/tenderness suggests DVT source
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Assessment 2e–f + History — exam includes skin/extremities; identifying source-of-clot supports the PE differential.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Exam: Airway/breathing (airway edema, rales, wheezing, pulse oximetry, respiratory rate). Circulation (heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill). Abdomen (hepatomegaly). Mucous membrane hydration. Skin (turgor, rash). Neurologic (GCS, sensorimotor deficits).

expectedActions.shortAction: Withhold NTG (preload-dependent — RV will collapse); withhold albuterol (no bronchospasm)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Patient Safety + Respiratory Distress — PE is preload-dependent; vasodilator nitrates can precipitate cardiovascular collapse. Albuterol is for bronchospasm pattern, which is absent here.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Etiology of respiratory distress: Bronchospastic etiology (e.g., asthma, COPD) is suggested by: Wheezing on auscultation; "Shark fin" waveform capnograph or prolonged expiratory phase; History of asthma/COPD.

transitions.if: Supportive O2 + cautious 250 mL bolus + rapid transport
transitions.condition: Crew gives high-flow O2 with rising SpO2, runs a small 250 mL test bolus to support preload, prepares norepinephrine drip in standby, and transports rapidly to a CTPA-capable ED with advance notification of suspected PE.

Crew anchored on 'pleuritic chest pain' and gave SL nitroglycerin without screening for PE risk factors or recognizing right-heart strain; vasodilation drops preload, the right ventricle decompensates, and the patient becomes acutely hypotensive.

phases.name: Supportive O2 + cautious bolus + rapid transport
phases.description: Crew applies high-flow O2 via NRB at 15 L/min; SpO2 rises to 92%. A small 250 mL NS test bolus is run to support RV preload (since the patient is hypotensive but preload-dependent). Norepinephrine drip is prepared in standby. The receiving ED is notified of high-suspicion PE for rapid CTPA. Transport is initiated upright-of-comfort with continuous monitoring; vitals reassessed q5 min en route.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew chose the supportive PE pathway: high-flow O2, cautious 250 mL test bolus to support preload, norepinephrine prepared in standby, advance notification of suspected PE, and rapid transport to CTPA-capable ED.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 26, still distressed but slightly improved with NRB; lungs clear; pleuritic pain unchanged
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulse 116 regular; warm; cap refill 2 sec
otherFindings: NRB at 15 L/min, SpO2 92% (up from 86%)
250 mL NS bolus run; lungs remain clear afterward
Norepinephrine drip primed and ready, not yet running
BP 102/66 — improved with O2 + bolus
Receiving ED notified: 'High suspicion PE, post-op, S1Q3T3, hypoxic, hypotensive — please prep CTPA'
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: The oxygen helps. It still hurts to breathe in but I'm getting more air.

expectedActions.shortAction: 250 mL NS test bolus; reassess lungs after — STOP if rales develop
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock fluid resuscitation + Patient Safety — boluses pressure-infused over <15 min; physiologic target MAP 65; withhold further fluid if condition deteriorates after bolus.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: IV fluid volume goal attained by giving boluses that are pressure infused over less than 15 minutes each based on patient's condition and clinical impression. Fluid volume goal to achieve a mean arterial pressure (adults). Adult: Physiologic target: MAP goal 65 mmHg. Patient Safety Considerations: Recognition of cardiogenic shock - If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration.

expectedActions.shortAction: Norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min ready in standby; start if MAP <65 after bolus
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Vasopressors 11a-i — norepi 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP 65 for fluid-refractory obstructive shock.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Vasopressors (shock unresponsive to IV fluids) titrated to physiologic targets: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock and distributive shock: Norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/minute.

expectedActions.shortAction: Advance notify ED: 'High-suspicion PE, post-op, S1Q3T3, hypoxic — prep CTPA'
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Treatment 13 — provide advanced notification to the hospital so receiving team can mobilize CT, anticoagulation, and possibly thrombolysis or interventional radiology.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Provide advanced notification to the hospital.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport in position of comfort (upright); minimize patient-driven activity (no walking)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 4a + Shock — position of comfort; avoid increased oxygen demand or sudden preload changes in suspected PE.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-pharmacologic pain management options include: Placement of the patient in a position of comfort. Application of ice packs and/or splints for pain secondary to trauma. Verbal reassurance to control anxiety.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess vitals + lung exam + EtCO2 trend q5 min; document trajectory for ED
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Documentation — full vitals q15 min or more frequently; document medications, fluids, neuro status, and response.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Medications administered. Full vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) with reassessment q 15 minutes or more frequently as appropriate. Amount of fluids given.

transitions.if: Stable on supportive care; transport to CTPA-capable ED
transitions.condition: Stable on high-flow O2 and cautious crystalloid; norepi in standby; advance notice given; transport to CTPA-capable ED with continuous monitoring and reassessment.

phases.name: Stable on supportive care; transport to CTPA ED
phases.description: Patient transported to a tertiary ED capable of CTPA, anticoagulation/thrombolysis, and interventional radiology. SpO2 maintained 92–95% on NRB. Norepinephrine remains in standby — never started — because the cautious bolus held the MAP. Continuous monitoring includes pulse ox, EtCO2, rhythm, and serial BPs every 5 minutes. Bedside handoff includes risk factors (post-op, immobility, obesity, prior tobacco), exam findings (clear lungs with hypoxia, right calf signs), 12-lead findings (S1Q3T3 + incomplete RBBB), and the rationale for each action withheld (no nitro, no albuterol, no diuretic).
phases.triggerCondition: Stable on supportive O2 + cautious crystalloid; transport active to CTPA-capable ED with advance notification of suspected PE.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular pulse 110; warm; cap refill 2 sec
otherFindings: Holding SpO2 92–95% on NRB
BP holding 100–108 systolic without pressors
Norepi remains primed in standby
Receiving ED has CT and pharmacy ready
Patient still pleuritic but stable
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I feel about the same. Glad we're moving. I just want to know what's wrong.

expectedActions.shortAction: Watch for sudden hypotension, syncope, or rhythm change — RV decompensation warning
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Documentation + Pertinent Findings — obstructive shock can decompensate abruptly; pulses, neuro status, perfusion are the early markers.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Decreased perfusion manifested by altered mental status, or abnormalities in capillary refill or pulses, decreased urine output (1 mL/kg/hr): Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock: capillary refill greater than 2 seconds, diminished peripheral pulses, mottled cool extremities.

expectedActions.shortAction: Defib pads on; sync mode pre-armed; ready for VT/VF or PEA from RV failure
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock — anticipate cardiac arrest with massive PE; pads support immediate response if rhythm collapses.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pertinent Assessment Findings: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock: capillary refill greater than 2 seconds, diminished peripheral pulses, mottled cool extremities.

expectedActions.shortAction: Bedside handoff: risk factors, S1Q3T3, withheld treatments, response to bolus + O2
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Documentation — full vitals trend, fluid total, medications administered, neurologic status, and history of event supporting differential.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Medications administered. Full vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) with reassessment q 15 minutes or more frequently as appropriate. Amount of fluids given.

phases.name: Nitroglycerin given for 'chest pain'
phases.description: Crew anchored on the words 'chest pain' from triage and gave SL nitroglycerin 0.4 mg before fully working through the differential. This patient's right ventricle is failing under acute pressure overload from the embolus and is critically preload-dependent — venodilation from nitrates dropped preload, the RV decompensated, and BP collapsed within 90 seconds. Patient becomes lightheaded, BP falls to 64/40, mentation clouds, and pulse becomes thready. The teaching point is that 'chest pain' is not a free pass to NTG — pleuritic pain with hypoxia, clear lungs, post-op risk factors, and right-heart strain on EKG should defer nitrates pending further workup. Crew now must support preload aggressively (cautious bolus + early norepinephrine), maintain oxygenation, and consider push-dose epi as a bridge while the drip is prepared.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew gave SL nitroglycerin before working through the PE differential; vasodilation precipitated RV collapse and acute hypotension — preload-dependent right ventricle decompensated under nitrate effect.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 36, shallow, splinting; lungs still clear
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Thready radial pulse; cool skin developing; cap refill 4 sec; right calf signs unchanged
otherFindings: NTG 0.4 mg SL given — BP fell within 90 seconds
BP 64/40 — RV decompensation under nitrate effect
Mentation clouding (verbal-only response)
EtCO2 dropped from 28 → 18 — falling perfusion
Lungs still clear (still PE pattern, not edema)
Bridge with push-dose epi while norepi drip is prepared
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (weak) I feel… really bad. Like I'm fading.

expectedActions.shortAction: 500 mL NS pressure bolus; reassess MAP — preload support post-nitro
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock fluid resuscitation — pressure-infused boluses titrated to MAP goal 65; appropriate response when MAP drops with iatrogenic vasodilation.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: IV fluid volume goal attained by giving boluses that are pressure infused over less than 15 minutes each based on patient's condition and clinical impression. Adult: Physiologic target: MAP goal 65 mmHg. Fluid goal of up to 30 mL/kg of isotonic fluid by administering rapid, predetermined boluses (e.g., 500 mL) unless the MAP goal is achieved, or pulmonary edema develops.

expectedActions.shortAction: Push-dose epi 10 mcg IV (1 mL of 10 mcg/mL dilution) bridge while norepi drip primed
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Vasopressors 11a-ii — push-dose epi 10 mcg q3–5 min as alternative/bridge to drip; useful in time-sensitive collapse.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Alternative to a drip, push dose epinephrine may be administered: Prepare 10 mcg/mL by diluting 1 mL of epinephrine 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000) in 9 mL of normal saline. Administer 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg) up to a maximum single dose of 10 mcg (1 mL) q 3–5 minutes titrated to maintain goal MAP.

expectedActions.shortAction: Start norepinephrine 5–10 mcg/min (≈0.07–0.13 mcg/kg/min for 75 kg) titrate MAP >65
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Vasopressors 11a-i — norepi 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP 65; preferred for fluid-refractory obstructive shock.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Vasopressors (shock unresponsive to IV fluids) titrated to physiologic targets: Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock and distributive shock: Norepinephrine 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/minute.

expectedActions.shortAction: BVM-assisted ventilations supplementing patient's own efforts; FiO2 100%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Treatment 1c — non-invasive ventilation/BVM for severe respiratory distress; SpO2 84 with deterioration mandates assist.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) should be administered for severe respiratory distress via BVM, continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) or bi-level positive airway pressure (BiPAP).

expectedActions.shortAction: Document anchoring error; debrief: 'pleuritic pain ≠ nitro' rule for PE pattern
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock + Pain Management — drug allergies/risk identified prior to administration; QI moment for differential discipline before nitrate dosing.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: All patients should have drug allergies identified prior to administration of pain medication. Administer opioids with caution to patients with Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) less than 15, hypotension, identified medication allergy, hypoxia (SPO2 less than 90%) after maximal supplemental oxygen therapy, or signs of hypoventilation.
